title: Why doctors leave medicine after a malpractice suit
summary: An excerpt from The Healthcare Heist: How Physicians and Patients Can Unite to Transform Healthcare. Every day, Francine said a little prayer for her doctor, sometimes upon awakening, often before dri...
full_content:
An excerpt from The Healthcare Heist: How Physicians and Patients Can Unite to Transform Healthcare.

Every day, Francine said a little prayer for her doctor, sometimes upon awakening, often before drifting off to sleep at night. Her world had narrowed significantly since Simon, her husband, passed away the year before. With no children or close friends, Francine relied heavily on Doctor G to make sure that her needs were met.

Initially, Francine enjoyed visiting the office and chatting with the receptionist. As Francine’s mobility declined, however, Doctor G began making house calls. It was not just the physical strain; the lingering depression following Simon’s death weighed heavily on her. The antidepressant pills, however, were beginning to take effect. The few colors she could still discern seemed brighter, and there was even talk of her niece visiting from Ohio.

Then came devastating news: Doctor G was retiring from medicine and leaving the practice. Francine had always imagined that because he was much younger than her previous doctor, he would care for her until the end.

Her last visit to the office was bittersweet. Though her physical condition had improved quite a bit, meeting Doctor G’s replacement left her feeling disheartened. The new doctor appeared hurried and less warm.

A few days later, as Francine experienced a brief bout of chest pain while watching television in her living room on a brisk Chicago day, she hesitated to call her new doctor. Outside, rooftops glistened with a fresh dusting of snow. Doctor G would have stopped by her apartment on the way out of the office and assessed her condition in the comfort of her own home. But with this new doctor, well, who could say?

As Francine drifted off to sleep, she pondered why such a young doctor would be leaving medicine so prematurely, and who would care for all of his elderly patients in the future. In fact, it was likely her last thought before slipping into unconsciousness. Perhaps she felt a fleeting pain or a moment of breathlessness as the massive heart attack overtook her. We will never know. We also will never know if Doctor G would have indeed visited her in the apartment or sent her to the hospital. In any number of alternate realities, she might have received appropriate and timely care and welcomed her niece into town with open arms a week later.

Instead, she died of a broken heart.

A promise to myself

Early in my medical training, a close friend faced unjust accusations of malpractice, enduring a grueling trial marked by self-doubt and eventual vindication. Witnessing her ordeal, I made a solemn vow to myself: If ever sued, guilty or not, justified or not, I would leave medicine without hesitation. The trauma of standing trial would destroy the will to practice medicine again.

The unspoken reality of being named in a lawsuit fundamentally alters how one practices the delicate art of medicine. Objectivity is lost, replaced by an instinct to overcorrect and protect oneself. The focus shifts from delivering the best care for the patient, to avoiding the trauma of being sued again. We become paranoid and overcautious.

The shift is supported by the data. A 2018 study from the National Bureau of Economic Research revealed that doctors’ fear of lawsuits increases the intensity and cost of hospital care by 5 percent, without yielding measurably better outcomes.

In my case, supposition became chilling reality one cold winter night when, at my front door, I was handed a summons to appear before a judge, while my wife and children sat quietly watching television in the adjacent family room. What ensued was a calamity spanning four years, costing my insurance company and me thousands of dollars in missed work and deductibles, ultimately leading to my departure from private practice and clinical medicine altogether.

The conclusion of the story was rather anticlimactic. After years of turmoil, I was dropped from the lawsuit when my deposition testimony finally persuaded the nursing home involved to settle with the deceased patient’s family. I received my own personal reprieve a few months earlier, before the settlement, when the plaintiffs’ attorney openly confessed in court to the judge that he had never intended to pursue the complaint against me. He had used my testimony as leverage to extract a larger settlement from the nursing home and hospital.

I was a connecting puzzle piece. A pawn. A resource to be exploited in the attorney’s high-stakes blame game. The attorney sensed that the roulette wheel of fate was poised to yield a significant payout.

The attorney positioned himself as an advocate for the patient and their family, but everyone involved understood the reality. The nursing home had committed no wrongdoing, an indisputable truth acknowledged by all parties. Economically, settling made more sense than proceeding to court regardless of the outcome, win or lose. The nursing home’s attorney, wise to the financial realities, recognized that prevailing in a protracted legal battle would exceed the costs of a settlement.

For the patient’s family, the settlement provided only modest compensation when compared to the years of anguish and extended grief caused by contentious litigation. No amount of money could replace their loved one. The past could not be undone. Meanwhile, the plaintiff’s attorney walked away with a substantial percentage of the settlement. In fact, he was the only one smiling at the end of this heartbreaking fiasco.

Collateral damage

By the way, I am Doctor G. Francine was one of my patients. My decision to leave medicine undoubtedly contributed to her premature death, as well as that of several other patients who passed away shortly after I closed my practice. It wasn’t just Francine. There was George, battling brittle diabetes for years, who was just barely hanging on before I left, and Sasha, prone to congestive heart failure exacerbations when she traveled out of town. Her new doctor, unfamiliar with her medical history, took a more lenient approach to her restrictions, not having been involved in her last hospital admission.

When doctors leave their practices, they take with them invaluable knowledge and expertise accumulated over years of caring for their patients, most of which has never been formally written down. This issue has been exacerbated even further in this post-pandemic era. According to a 2022 report by Definitive Healthcare, 334,000 health care providers exited the workforce in 2021, including 117,000 doctors. The primary care specialties suffered especially significant losses, with internal medicine seeing a departure of 15,000 MDs and family practice losing 13,000 practitioners.

Patients are not the only ones affected. When I left my practice, the livelihoods of my staff, secretaries, lab techs, and others, were also impacted. Physicians often drive the revenue for medical offices, and without their presence, the financial resources to cover salaries and essential services dwindle.

Attributing the entire great resignation and physician shortage solely to the malpractice crisis would be premature. However, it is undeniable that concerns and anxieties related to malpractice contribute significantly to burnout and frustration among physicians. In fact, psychiatrists recently coined the term medical malpractice stress syndrome, or MMSS, to describe the traumatic aftermath physicians endure following malpractice lawsuits. Those affected by MMSS often suffer through isolation, helplessness, and hopelessness, which can increase the risk of depression and even suicide. It also is a primary driver in the decision to leave medicine.

Jordan Grumet is an internal medicine physician.

title: Canada’s primary care investment is missing a North Star
summary: Something remarkable is happening in Canadian health policy right now. After decades of incremental change, provinces and territories are making serious, sustained investments in primary care. These b...
full_content:
Something remarkable is happening in Canadian health policy right now. After decades of incremental change, provinces and territories are making serious, sustained investments in primary care. These bilateral health agreements represent a genuine federal-provincial commitment, billions of dollars directed toward the kind of team-based, patient-centered care that Canadians have been asking for.

The people designing these investments care deeply. The intent is right. But when the accountability reviews come, how will we know if it worked?

An unfortunate pattern has emerged across Canadian health systems that is worth naming. When a province sets out to transform primary care, it consults international models found outside of Canada, Alaska’s Nuka System, England’s Primary Care Networks, Australia’s Primary Health Networks. It convenes researchers, hires consultants, builds something thoughtful and locally responsive.

And then the province next door does the same thing. Starting fresh. Learning the same lessons from international models but not from models within our own country. Building something no one in Canada can easily learn from.

This is not a failure of effort or intention. It is what happens when a system navigates without a shared reference point, when flexibility, which is genuinely valuable, has no common language alongside it.

Canada’s bilateral agreements are designed to honor provincial autonomy. That is right and appropriate. Provinces should choose their own paths. But Canadian provinces and territories need a North Star for primary care, a common definition of what good primary care looks like for patients, that sits alongside provincial and territorial flexibility rather than constraining it.

This is not a new idea. We have built this North Star infrastructure in other sectors without controversy. The National Building Code does not tell a contractor how to pour a foundation. It tells them what the foundation must accomplish. Provinces adopt it, adapt it, and enforce it as they choose. But a building inspector in Halifax and one in Vancouver are working from shared expectations about what safe means.

Aviation safety standards do not prescribe how airlines operate. They define what safe flight requires. The result is that air travel across Canada, and across the world, is built on common ground.

Health care in Canada has never been treated this way. Not because the idea is wrong, but because the political and historical conditions have never quite aligned. They may be aligning now.

Canada already has promising foundations to build from.

First, the Health Standards Organization’s (HSO) Primary Health Care Services Standard, currently in public review until April 29, defines quality primary care around what patients experience: whether they can access care when they need it, whether that care is continuous, comprehensive, and coordinated.

Second, the OurCare initiative has produced rich, nationally grounded insight into what Canadians actually want from their primary care system. Third, the College of Family Physicians of Canada has shared its Patient’s Medical Home vision.

These efforts represent years of careful work. They do not need to compete. They could, together, inform a shared reference point, a North Star, that any province, health authority, or practice team could voluntarily draw on as they navigate their own transformation.

A North Star for primary care would not be a mandate. It would not be a new reporting burden. It would not be a federal imposition on provincial jurisdiction. It would provide a common language, a shared departure point, and a way of ensuring that what we learn in Nova Scotia can be understood and built upon in British Columbia.

Federal-provincial bilateral accountability reviews are underway. Three-year action plans expire in 2026-27 and renewal decisions are imminent, just as early data reveals that provinces may be falling short of their own targets and commitments. Decision-makers will be asked to renew billions in funding again without a shared definition of what good primary care looks like for patients. That gap is worth closing now.

The federally commissioned FPT Primary Care Working Group is well positioned to explore this. So are the provinces themselves, if given the tools to share implementation work. National organizations, such as the Health Standards Organization (HSO), and the new collaboration between Healthcare Excellence Canada (HEC) and the Canadian Institute for Health Research (CIHR), offer real opportunities for more aligned national progress, if encouraged to work together.

Leadership for transformation need not be top-down. But it does require leaders at all levels willing to speak a common language and move in a shared direction.

Canada already has the evidence. We have the goodwill. We also have a once-in-a-generation primary care investment underway. What we need now is a shared sense of where we are going, together.

The route is up to each province and territory. But every journey is easier with a North Star.

Ivy Oandasan is a family physician in Canada.

title: The corporatization of medicine, in salaries and beds
summary: In the 1970s, the American hospital was, in the industry’s own language, an “open workshop.” A physician kept an office down the street, admitted patients to the hospital when they n...
full_content:
In the 1970s, the American hospital was, in the industry’s own language, an “open workshop.” A physician kept an office down the street, admitted patients to the hospital when they needed a bed or an operating room, and worked largely independent of whoever managed the building. The doctor made the medical decisions; the administrator kept the lights on and the ledgers balanced. It was an arrangement with obvious flaws (fragmentation, duplication, uneven quality), but it rested on a clear premise: Medicine was practiced by physicians, and the institution existed to support that practice.

That premise has quietly inverted. The hospital is no longer a workshop that doctors visit. It is a corporation that employs them. The practice down the street has been acquired, rebranded, and folded into a system with a marketing department, a real-estate strategy, and a chief executive whose compensation would have been unimaginable to the physicians of the open-workshop era. The people who now set the direction of American medicine are, increasingly, not the people who see patients.

This is not a story about villains. Modern medicine is genuinely more complex than it was fifty years ago, and some of the administrative apparatus that has grown up around it is the price of that complexity: electronic records, safety reporting, cybersecurity, the labyrinth of insurance. But complexity does not explain everything, and it has become a convenient justification for a shift in power that has its own logic and its own costs. The question worth asking is not whether hospitals need managers. They do. The question is what happens to a caring profession when the managers are in charge.

The great inversion: who owns the practice

The clearest way to see the change is in who owns the place where care happens. In the early 1980s, roughly three-quarters of American physicians had an ownership stake in their practice, according to a 1996 estimate from Kletke and colleagues; they were, in a real sense, the proprietors of the medical system. By 2012, that share had fallen to 53 percent. By 2024, only about 35 percent of physicians held any ownership stake at all, according to the American Medical Association’s Physician Practice Benchmark Survey.

Period Physicians with an ownership stake
Early 1980s 76 percent
2012 53 percent
2024 35 percent

The mirror image of that decline is employment. As of January 2024, a report by the Physicians Advocacy Institute and Avalere found that 77.6 percent of U.S. physicians were employed by a hospital, health system, or other corporate entity, a figure that had climbed relentlessly for more than a decade. More than 500,000 doctors were on those payrolls. In the same analysis, corporate ownership of physician practices (by insurers, pharmacy chains, and private-equity firms) surpassed hospital ownership for the first time, 30.1 percent to 28.4 percent. By the institute’s 2026 update, nearly 64 percent of all physician practices were owned by someone other than a physician.

The reasons doctors gave up ownership are real and sympathetic: declining reimbursement, the crushing cost of running a small business, the regulatory and billing burden, burnout. Many were relieved to hand those headaches to someone else. But relief is not the same as control. When a physician becomes an employee, the terms of medicine (the schedule, the productivity targets, the electronic templates, the referral patterns, the choice of which services to offer) are increasingly set by people whose training and incentives are managerial rather than clinical.

An army of administrators: the growth of management

As ownership migrated, so did the workforce. The most-cited figure in this debate comes from Physicians for a National Health Program, which calculated that between 1975 and 2010 the number of U.S. physicians grew about 150 percent, roughly in step with population, while the number of health care administrators grew 3,200 percent. The chart built from that statistic has become a fixture of every lecture on what ails American medicine.

Workforce Growth, 1975 to 2010
Physicians 150 percent
Administrators 3,200 percent

It deserves a caveat, and the caveat matters. Stricter reads of the federal labor data put the growth of health care managers closer to 600 to 1,100 percent over comparable periods, enormous, but not 3,200. Some of the gap is definitional: The raw category sweeps in clerks, coders, billing staff, and compliance officers, not just executives. But even the conservative numbers tell the same story. Administrative roles have expanded several times faster than the clinical workforce, and federal projections expect medical-and-health-services-manager jobs to grow 28 percent between 2022 and 2032, against 3 percent for physicians.

In the most rigorous international comparison to date, a study led by David Himmelstein in Health Affairs found that administration consumed 25.3 percent of total U.S. hospital spending, the highest of eight nations studied, and more than double the share in Scotland or Canada. For-profit hospitals ran even higher, at 27.2 percent. A 2021 analysis in JAMA estimated that the United States spends on the order of a trillion dollars a year on health care administration, roughly a quarter of all health spending, and the American Hospital Association’s own 2026 blueprint concedes the figure now sits between 25 and 35 percent.

Crucially, that spending buys little in the way of better care. Himmelstein’s team found “no apparent link between higher administrative costs and better-quality care.” The overhead is not a proxy for excellence. It is, to a substantial degree, the cost of running medicine as a business that must bill, negotiate, market, and grow.

The corner office: executive compensation

Nowhere is the reordering of priorities more visible than at the top of the pay scale. The chief executive of a nonprofit hospital system (an institution that pays no taxes because it exists, in law, to serve its community) now earns on average about $1.3 million a year, according to research from Rice University’s Baker Institute. That is the average. The distribution runs far higher. In 2018, Bernard Tyson of Kaiser Permanente was paid nearly $18 million, making him the highest-paid nonprofit executive of any kind in the country. The Lown Institute has documented nonprofit hospital chiefs earning north of $30 million in a single year. Even the bottom quartile of nonprofit hospital CEOs out-earns the top quartile of executives running nonprofit arts, environmental, and human-services organizations.

The trajectory is as striking as the level. Between 2005 and 2015, the average compensation of major nonprofit hospital CEOs rose 93 percent, from roughly $1.6 million to $3.1 million, according to a Lown Institute analysis. Over the same decade, Economic Policy Institute data show the wages of the average hospital worker rose about 8 percent. During the first year of the COVID-19 pandemic, when nurses and aides were furloughed or sent to the front lines, more than 80 percent of hospitals kept paying their CEOs bonuses. In one widely reported case, a health-system chief who never touched a ventilator received a 13 percent raise, bringing his package to $30.4 million.

Compensation Increase, 2005 to 2015
Nonprofit hospital CEOs 93 percent
Average hospital workers 8 percent

How does this happen inside charities? The mechanism is now well understood. Hospital boards set executive pay by benchmarking against “comparable” organizations, but the comparison group is no longer other community nonprofits. It is for-profit hospital chains, which in turn benchmark against the largest industrial and financial companies on Wall Street. Researchers writing in Health Affairs called it “an ever-spiraling upward cycle.” The boards doing the setting are themselves dominated by business: One 2023 study of fifteen top-ranked hospitals found that more than half of board members came from finance or business, and just 15 percent had any clinical training.

And what are these executives paid to do? The Rice study is blunt on the point. CEOs were rewarded, above all, for the size and profitability of their organizations; leading a system with 500 or more beds was associated with 157 percent higher pay than running one with fewer than 100. The link between compensation and actual quality of care, measured by readmissions and mortality, was weak to begin with and grew weaker over the study period. The health economist Vivian Ho, who led the work, found that fatter margins did not translate into more charity care. The incentive, she argued, rewards CEOs for maintaining a lucrative status quo, not for improving a community’s health.

What gets measured: mission drift

Follow the incentive and you can predict the behavior. If a chief executive’s pay tracks the number of beds and the size of the margin, then beds and margin are what the institution will pursue. This is the quiet engine behind the phenomena physicians complain about most: the relentless drive for volume, especially of well-insured patients; the mergers that produce ever-larger systems with ever-greater negotiating leverage; and the building.

Every clinician knows the pattern. The health system announces a gleaming new tower, a rebranded logo, a naming-rights deal, a lobby with a grand piano, while the units upstairs run short of nurses, and the request for another staff physician is denied as “not in the budget.” The capital flows to the things that photograph well and generate revenue: the proton-beam center, the destination cardiac program, the suburban outpatient campus in a wealthy zip code. It flows less readily to the unglamorous work of staffing a ward, keeping a rural clinic open, or caring for patients who cannot pay. A facility is an asset on a balance sheet and a monument to a tenure. Adequate staffing is a recurring expense. Management, rewarded for growth, tends to prefer the asset.

The deepest evidence that this is a governance problem, not merely the inevitable price of complexity, comes from a simple comparison: who runs the hospital. Research by the economist Amanda Goodall found that hospitals led by physicians posted overall quality scores roughly 25 percent higher than those led by professional managers, and for cancer care the gap reached 33 percent. There is, meanwhile, no evidence that installing an MBA-trained chief executive improves outcomes at all. When the person at the top has stood at a bedside, the institution’s priorities appear to bend, at least somewhat, back toward the bedside.

The cost of being managed: consequences at the bedside

For the physician, the shift from owner to employee has a name that is only now entering the literature: administrative harm. Clinicians increasingly describe decisions being made, as one put it to MedPage Today, “by people very far from patients,” with little accountability compared to the accountability a doctor carries for a clinical mistake. A staffing ratio set in a spreadsheet, a prior-authorization protocol, a productivity quota, an electronic-record template designed for billing rather than thinking: Each is an administrative act with clinical consequences, made by someone who will never be sued for it and never meet the patient it affects.

The result is a profession in distress. The documentation burden that administration was supposed to relieve has, for many physicians, only grown, feeding an epidemic of burnout that pushes experienced doctors toward early retirement, part-time work, or exit from clinical practice altogether, which in turn worsens the very staffing shortages that management declines to fund. It is a loop that runs in one direction, and patients feel it as longer waits, shorter visits, and the sense, increasingly common, that the person across the desk is being hurried by forces neither of them controls.

When the math stops working: supply, demand, and the shrinking margin

There is a further twist, and it may prove the most consequential of all: The economics that made the takeover attractive are now turning against it. The country faces a worsening physician shortage: too few doctors trained, an aging workforce retiring, and demand rising as the population grays. Basic supply and demand does the rest. When a scarce resource is in high demand, its price climbs, and physician compensation has done exactly that. Hospital finance officers now cite workforce shortages as the basic driver pushing pay up; consulting firm SullivanCotter found physicians’ median cash compensation recently grew faster than it had in a decade, with primary-care pay up nearly 22 percent over five years and some specialties jumping more than 7 percent in a single year.

Employers, in other words, must pay more each year simply to keep the doctors they have. What they collect for those doctors’ work, however, is moving the other way. Medicare physician payment has fallen roughly 29 percent from 2001 to 2024 once adjusted for inflation in practice costs, according to the American Medical Association. A further 2.8 percent cut took effect in 2025, and even the one-time 2.5 percent increase Congress passed for 2026 does not keep pace with rising practice costs. Commercial insurers, whose rates often track Medicare, have shown little appetite to close the gap. The revenue per physician is flat or shrinking while the cost of employing one keeps climbing.

The shift also quietly rewired who fights for the money. When doctors owned their practices, they fought the payers directly; every fee schedule was their fight, and a Medicare or insurer cut landed on them. Employed physicians have largely stepped out of that ring. They no longer negotiate fees with insurers at all; they negotiate a salary and a per-unit RVU rate with their employer. Their income is increasingly decoupled from what the payer actually reimburses, which means the employer now stands between the two, absorbing the difference between the compensation the market forces it to offer and the fees the payers are willing to pay. Having taken control, in other words, management also inherited the fee fight, and the burden of finding the money to close the gap.

That is a scissor, and it cuts straight through the business case for owning practices. It is an open secret in the industry that hospital-owned physician practices generally operate at a loss: The American Medical Group Association puts the median loss at just under $250,000 per employed physician per year. Systems have papered over the gap by billing acquired offices at the richer “hospital outpatient department” rate, but most experts expect those premiums to erode as regulators tighten the rules, and when they do, more practices will be cut loose. The tell is already in the data: In 2024 and 2025, corporate owners kept buying practices yet grew their physician headcount by less than 1 percent, prompting the question of whether they are quietly shedding the doctors they acquired.

And here the systems confront a bind they are only now fully appreciating. On the ledger, the employed physician is a cost center, an expensive, loss-making line. But that accounting hides where the money actually starts. Almost nothing in a hospital bills without a physician’s order behind it: the imaging study, the laboratory panel, the admission, the procedure, the prescription, the referral to a lucrative specialist. The doctor is not merely one revenue source among many; the doctor is the origin of the revenue, the signature that sets the entire billing machine in motion. Cut the physicians to trim the loss, and the tests, the beds, and the operating rooms downstream go quiet with them. That is the lesson management is learning the hard way: The one input it cannot economize away is the same input growing most expensive.

The irony is sharp. Management consolidated medicine on the promise of efficiency and scale, but it bought into a market where its central input, the physician, was about to become both scarcer and more expensive, while the price it could charge for that physician’s labor was being held down by payers. Layoffs, service-line closures, and the first wave of practice “unwinds” are the early symptoms. None of this restores physician control on its own. But it does mean the model that displaced the independent doctor is now under financial strain of its own making, and strained systems, sooner or later, are forced to reconsider what they are actually for.

Reclaiming the workshop: a way back

None of this argues for abolishing hospital administration, which would be both impossible and foolish; the complexity that management navigates is real, and some of it protects patients. The argument is narrower and more serious. It is that control has shifted too far, that the incentives at the top have detached from the mission at the bottom, and that a system nominally devoted to healing is increasingly steered by the metrics of growth.

What would refocusing look like? It would mean tying executive pay to measures of community health and care quality rather than beds and margins. It would mean boards that include the people who deliver care, not only the people who finance it. It would mean transparency, from publicly owned hospitals in particular, about what executives earn and what quality they deliver in return. And it would mean taking seriously the finding that doctor-led institutions perform better, not as an argument that only physicians may manage, but as evidence that clinical judgment belongs in the room where the institution’s direction is set.

The open workshop of the 1970s is not coming back, nor should everything about it. But its premise is worth recovering: that the institution exists to serve the care, and not the other way around. The doctors who gave up ownership did so mostly out of exhaustion, one practice at a time. Getting the profession’s voice back into the corner office will take the opposite: a deliberate, collective insistence that medicine is still, at bottom, something that happens between a clinician and a patient, and that everyone else in the building is there to make that possible.

Brian Hudes is a board-certified gastroenterologist with more than 30 years of clinical experience, serving as chief of gastroenterology and medical director of GI and endoscopy at Ascension Sacred Heart Hospital in Pensacola, Florida, a 550-bed Level I trauma center, and as assistant professor of medicine at Florida State University College of Medicine. A recipient of his specialty board’s 30-year certification award, he has spent his career at the intersection of complex clinical care and the structural forces that shape how medicine is practiced, financed, and delivered.

Dr. Hudes brings a rare dual perspective to health care commentary: that of a frontline proceduralist who has navigated decades of declining reimbursement, rising administrative burden, and accelerating system consolidation, and that of a health care technology entrepreneur who has spent years studying why the systems around medicine so often fail the people practicing it. His health care IT work began during his GI fellowship in 1995, when he co-developed one of the first Windows-based endoscopy reporting systems in the United States.

Having practiced through every era of modern health care technology, from paper charts and handwritten orders to early electronic health records and today’s enterprise systems, Dr. Hudes writes with a grounded perspective on administrative cost growth, physician workforce shortages, end-of-life ethics, and the widening gap between what clinicians need and what the industry builds. Professional updates are available on LinkedIn.

title: Why AI scribes keep senior physicians in practice
summary: For years now, the burnout conversation in health care has been almost exclusively fixated on mid-career physicians, who are staring down decades of EHR documentation and quietly refreshing their Link...
full_content:
For years now, the burnout conversation in health care has been almost exclusively fixated on mid-career physicians, who are staring down decades of EHR documentation and quietly refreshing their LinkedIn profiles. These exhausted doctors in their prime are considering early exits, career pivots, and anything to get out from under the weight of it all. However, there’s another story playing out, quieter and in some ways more striking, among physicians in years 25 through 35 of their careers.

These are the ones who have shouldered the charting burden for so long that it became part of the job description. Not something to fix, just something to endure. For them, ambient AI scribes aren’t just a productivity tool, but closer to a liberation, which is capable of removing what many describe as a “boat anchor” that has been following them through most of their professional lives.

As the CEO of a clinical documentation company, I’ve heard this directly and repeatedly from clinicians. Late-career physicians who had genuinely, quietly accepted that documentation would never get better. They powered through because that’s what dedication looked like, because there was no other option. And then AI scribes came along, and something happened that nobody was really tracking: They started having the most fun they’ve ever had in medicine.

That’s not the headline anybody’s writing.

Workforce retention conversations obsess over preventing mid-stream departures, and physician shortages are real, and losing talent mid-career disrupts care continuity in ways that take years to recover from. On the other hand, I would argue that a more transformative outcome might actually be the re-engagement of our most experienced clinicians. Seasoned physicians carry irreplaceable characteristics: clinical instincts refined over decades, deep referral networks, high procedural volumes, and the kind of diagnostic pattern recognition you simply cannot shortcut. Keeping them energized and in practice longer is a strategic multiplier that most health systems aren’t modeling for at all.

A hidden revolution in burnout data

There’s a landmark study from Yale New Haven Health that evaluated 263 clinicians across six health systems. The findings revealed that baseline burnout rates fell from 51.9 percent to 38.8 percent after just 30 days of ambient AI scribe use. That’s a 74 percent reduction in the adjusted odds of burnout in just 30 days.

The data also showed significant drops in cognitive task load and after-hours documentation time, or what clinicians call “pajama time,” the notes you write at midnight because the day didn’t leave room. Furthermore, there were measurable improvements in patient focus and care access. People reported an immediate drop in note-related mental strain, which freed them to actually be present during appointments in a way that, for some of them, hadn’t happened in years.

For late-career clinicians who had long stopped expecting any of this to change, the relief doesn’t just land as incremental improvement. After 25 years of charting nights and weekends, suddenly reclaiming that time and that mental bandwidth feels revolutionary.

Health systems’ ROI models for AI scribes almost never capture this. You can build a spreadsheet for documentation time saved or maybe revenue from additional patient volume. Modeling tenure extension, which is the real financial and clinical value of a senior specialist practicing two, three, or five years longer than they otherwise would have, is rarely in the calculation. Research by the American Medical Association puts the organizational cost of replacing a single physician somewhere between $500,000 and over $1 million when you factor in recruitment, sign-on bonuses, the revenue vacuum during vacancies, and the ramp-up time to rebuild patient momentum. Across the system, burnout-driven churn and early retirement cost the U.S. health care system an estimated $4.6 billion annually. For senior specialists, the real loss runs even higher because of everything entrenched in those referral networks and procedural relationships.

The power of time reclaimed

Documentation savings hit differently depending on how someone practices. Independent benchmarks of ambient AI scribe deployments documented a 26.8 percent reduction in documentation time. For a primary care physician managing a 15 to 20 patient panel, that’s meaningful, and even transformative for a specialist seeing 40 or more patients daily, or a proceduralist whose schedule has no slack built in. When speed is taken care of, physicians can now document actual clinical nuance, given how complex cases get coded and reimbursed, and for the systemic under-reporting problem that’s been baked into clinical documentation for a long time.

For tenured physicians specifically, this reclaimed time is most often a renewed presence with patients, with colleagues, and sometimes with their families. A lot of them describe rediscovering why they got into medicine in the first place, which is not a small thing. Now, with their time being freed for the better part, they are capable of mentoring younger physicians more hands-on, taking on complex cases, and extending careers because practice feels sustainable again rather than a slow countdown.

That re-engagement creates ripple effects that go well beyond any individual physician. When an institution possesses a stable team with experienced clinicians, this will improve quality metrics and carry institutional memory across transitions. Their presence helps train the next generation and provides the kind of care continuity that patients, especially older, sicker patients with long histories, genuinely depend on.

Time to rethink the ROI conversation

It is essential that health care leaders expand how they’re evaluating AI scribe investments. Beyond per-note efficiency, the retention and re-engagement dividend needs to be taken seriously into the calculation, especially for physicians who might otherwise start winding down. What is the actual value of a senior cardiologist staying three more years? How much institutional knowledge and revenue gets preserved when a veteran surgeon doesn’t accelerate their retirement by a decade?

The tenured physician story asks us to see AI not just as a burnout mitigator for younger, newer doctors, but as a retention tool for the people who have given the most to this profession.

Technology is finally delivering the relief that was promised, and quietly, something real is happening among our most experienced clinicians. It’s probably time we started paying attention to that.

Pat Williams is a health care executive.

title: 心率、血糖之后，下一代医疗数据入口在哪？牛津团队瞄准连续代谢监测
summary: 随着智能手表与连续血糖监测（CGM）的普及，心率、血氧等物理信号及单一血糖数据已实现连续采集，推动健康管理从医院延伸至家庭。然而，现有数据多为反映生理变化的“结果指标”，难以触及人体内部早期代谢改变，且CGM仅覆盖复杂代谢网络的一维。牛津团队指出，葡萄糖、乳酸、酮体、NAD⁺等代谢分子能更直接反映能量利用、运动恢复、疾病进展及衰老过程，被视为未来精准医疗、AI医疗及个体化管理的核心基础。行业竞争正...
full_content:
过去几年，从智能手表、连续血糖监测（CGM）到各类可穿戴医疗设备，一场围绕人体健康数据入口的竞争打得火热。

 

越来越多企业希望通过持续采集人体数据，将疾病管理从医院延伸到家庭，将健康管理从间断体检延伸到全天候监测。从苹果、华为等消费电子企业，到雅培、Dexcom等医疗器械企业，再到众多数字医疗创业公司，都在试图回答同一个问题：未来健康管理需要哪些数据，又该如何获得这些数据？

 

竞争带来进步。当前，心率、血氧、睡眠、运动、体温等指标已经能够实现连续采集，连续血糖监测（CGM）更推动糖尿病管理进入实时监测时代。人体健康数据正以前所未有的速度被数字化。

 

但随着AI医疗和精准健康管理不断发展，新的问题开始浮现：数据越来越多，真正能够反映人体生理状态的数据却依然有限。

 

以智能手表为代表的消费级设备，大多采集的是心率、体温、血氧、运动等物理信号，它们能够反映人体状态的变化，却往往是“结果指标”——当心率升高、体温异常时，人体内部的代谢变化早已发生。即便是数字医疗最成熟的CGM，能够覆盖的也只是人体复杂代谢网络中的一个维度。

 

相比之下，葡萄糖、乳酸、酮体、NAD⁺等代谢分子，能更直接地反映人体能量利用、运动恢复、疾病进展乃至衰老过程，因此被越来越多研究机构认为是未来精准医疗、AI医疗和个体化健康管理的重要数据基础。

 

换句话说，过去行业竞争的是谁能够采集更多健康数据，那么未来或许是谁能够持续获取更多高价值的代谢数据。而这，也成为近年来全球生物传感领域开始共同探索的新方向。

 

“数据入口”到“数据价值”，医疗健康竞争正在进入下一阶段

 

连续健康监测的发展，是一部人体数据不断丰富的历史。

 

最早，可穿戴设备关注的是运动数据；随后，心率、睡眠、血氧等生理指标陆续实现连续监测；近年来，以CGM为代表的连续血糖监测快速普及，让代谢数据真正走出医院，进入日常生活。

 

CGM的成功，不仅改变了糖尿病患者的管理方式，也让行业看到连续代谢监测的巨大价值。

 

相比传统指尖采血只能反映某一时间点的数据，连续监测能够记录人体全天候的动态变化。无论是餐后血糖波动、运动后的代谢变化，还是夜间低血糖风险，都能够被完整记录下来。这种连续数据正在成为临床决策、慢病管理乃至人工智能算法训练的重要基础。

 

也正因如此，过去几年，越来越多企业开始尝试把连续监测从血糖扩展到更多代谢指标。例如，运动医学希望实时了解乳酸变化，以评估运动强度和恢复水平；生酮饮食管理需要连续监测血酮，以判断机体是否进入酮症状态；抗衰老研究则越来越关注NAD⁺等代谢分子的动态变化，希望借此更准确地评价细胞能量代谢和衰老进程。

 

这些需求共同指向一个趋势：多维度、连续性的代谢数据，正在成为下一代健康管理的重要基础设施。

 

然而诚如前文所述，真正能够实现连续、多指标代谢监测的产品却十分有限。因为大多数连续生物传感产品是围绕单一代谢物设计。无论是葡萄糖、乳酸还是酮体，不同代谢分子的检测机理、识别酶、反应环境以及信号处理方式都存在明显差异，这意味着每增加一个检测指标，往往需要重新设计传感体系、重新优化算法，甚至重新开发整套产品。不仅开发周期长、研发成本高，也很难实现不同指标之间的平台化复用。并且，各家头部企业在血糖单指标上已经建立了极高的技术壁垒和品牌护城河，因此更多是在持续优化既有指标。

 

这也是为什么连续血糖监测已经形成成熟市场，而连续乳酸、连续血酮等产品却迟迟没有迎来大规模商业化。

 

一支来自牛津大学体系的团队——津合生物，选择了一条截然不同的技术路线。

 

津合生物的核心技术为“酶电偶联”，其本质是基于直接电子传递的第三代酶电传感技术：通过纳米材料固定化酶，在酶的活性中心与电极之间构建一条高速电子通道，使酶与电极直接交换电子，无需依赖电子媒介体。

 

这一架构的关键突破在于：不同代谢分子虽然识别它们的酶各不相同，但最终输出的都是统一的电信号。这意味着，从血糖到血酮、从乳酸到NAD⁺，监测不同指标之间不再是“重新设计一套系统”，而是“换一个识别模块（酶）”即可。津合生物希望以此打造一套能够兼容不同代谢物检测的底层技术平台，实现多指标监测的平台化扩展。

 


津合生物核心团队来自牛津大学生物电化学研究体系，其技术源头可追溯至牛津大学长期积累的酶电化学研究。

 

牛津大学长期是全球生物电化学领域的重要研究中心之一。上世纪，Allen Hill等学者推动了酶电化学在生物传感领域的早期发展；近十年来，Fraser Armstrong院士持续深耕酶直接电子传递（DET）等基础研究，为新一代酶电传感技术提供了重要理论基础。

 

津合生物尝试将这些基础研究进一步工程化，并形成一套可扩展的生物传感平台。

 

简单来说，传统传感器像是一把只能开启一把锁的钥匙，每检测一种新的代谢物，都需要重新设计一整套识别与信号转换系统。这正是国际巨头们花了二十年攻克血糖、却在血酮和乳酸面前止步的根本原因：从葡萄糖氧化酶到乳酸氧化酶，酶的活性中心结构不同，电子传递路径不同，意味着电极材料、固定化方式、信号读取电路几乎要全部重来。

 

而津合生物建立的是一套统一的底层传感架构：不同代谢分子虽然仍然依靠不同的酶进行识别，但最终通过相对统一的电子信号输出。换言之，津合生物将传感过程拆解为“前端识别”与“后端信号输出”两个相对独立的模块，前端可以灵活更换，后端保持统一。


这意味着，新增一种代谢指标，不再需要重新开发整套传感器系统，而只需在已有平台上完成前端生物识别模块的开发与适配。这不仅大幅缩短了产品开发周期，也让平台具备了向更多应用场景持续扩展的能力。

 

而这种技术能力，在商业上意味着三件事：

 

首先，它能以极快的速度和较低的边际成本覆盖多个场景。以血糖监测为基础，平台可以进一步增加血酮、乳酸等检测能力，逐步覆盖糖尿病管理、生酮饮食、运动健康、康复监测等不同市场。每增加一个指标，都是在打开一个垂直市场，而新增的开发成本远低于“每做一个指标就造一条产线”的传统模式。

 

其次，它可以把传感器做成“平台”。无论是想做代谢监测的穿戴品牌，还是需要实时数据流的AI制药公司——都不必自己从头研发传感器。津合提供标准化的传感模块，客户按需选择监测指标，津合在几周内完成酶的组合配置与校准。这与云计算的逻辑相似：客户不需要自建服务器，只需按需调用算力；在津合的平台上，客户按需调用的是代谢数据采集能力。

 

最后，它能使数据资产化。当足够多的传感器铺出去，连续、多维度的代谢数据回流到云端，这套数据集的价值会超过硬件本身。保险公司的差异化定价、保健品公司的功效验证、长寿诊所的代谢年龄评估……这些商业模式的共性需求，都是高质量的真实世界代谢数据。而目前，几乎没有渠道能够持续提供这种数据。

 

随着商业价值不断拓展，津合生物也获得了资本市场的持续关注。2026年新一轮融资，产业方和国资已经率先入局。此前，公司已完成多轮融资，投资方包括奇绩创坛、中信资本、英诺基金、国宏嘉信、天择资本、鼎晖投资等机构。

 

 

三类不同行业的需求，指向了同一个答案——津合生物的平台化技术能够解决各自的数据监测难题。但这个答案的终点，或许会比想象中的要更加壮美辽阔。

 

今天的健康管理，依赖的是体检单上孤零零的箭头和零散的指标。血压高了吃降压药，血糖高了打胰岛素——这是一种“事后响应”的逻辑。但当血糖、血酮、乳酸、NAD⁺这些维度的数据流持续汇聚，再交给AI去拆解、关联、发现规律，它会拼出一张从未有过的东西：一张属于每一个人的动态代谢图谱。

 

这张图谱能回答的问题，远比“血糖高不高”深刻得多：你的能量系统怎么运转？脂肪代谢在什么水平？细胞的修复节奏是快是慢？相比实际年龄，你的代谢系统到底老了还是年轻了？

 

当足够多的传感器铺出去，每天回传数百万条数据——不同年龄、不同体质、不同生活方式的人汇在一起——就不再是一个人的画像，而是一整代人的身体数据底稿。

 

这份底稿的商业想象力远远超出了传感器本身。保险公司可以用它设计更精准的定价模型：一个代谢年龄比实际年龄年轻五岁的人，保费是否应该更低？保健品公司可以用它验证产品功效：你吃的那瓶NMN，NAD⁺到底涨没涨？长寿诊所可以把代谢年龄作为核心评估指标，像今天的体检报告一样普及。

 

这些不是遥远的未来。它们只缺一样东西：连续、多维度、低成本的代谢数据采集能力。

 

而这恰恰是津合生物正在交付的东西。

 

数据入口的战争已经打到了每个人的皮肤表面，但大家争抢的几乎都是同一类“矿物”——心率、体温、步数这些物理信号数据。真正有价值“矿脉”埋在地下更深的地方，而第一把能同时开采多种高价值“矿物”的铲子，已经由津合递出来……

title: 里程碑｜全球首款！无创神经调控设备切入卒中ICU，斩获FDA突破性认定
summary: 美国医疗企业Aurenar旗下V-Link非侵入性神经调节系统获FDA突破性认定，用于成人动脉瘤性蛛网膜下腔出血（aSAH）患者，可降低脑血管痉挛风险，是全球首款针对该重症场景获此认定的无创神经调控设备。aSAH是凶险急性脑卒中病症，出血后3至14天为脑血管痉挛高发期，发病率30%-70%，会致脑组织缺血、二次脑损伤，目前临床干预手段有限，唯一指南推荐药物尼莫地平无法从根源阻断炎症且副作用大，有创...
full_content:
近日，美国创新医疗企业Aurenar宣布旗下V-Link非侵入性神经调节系统成功获得FDA突破性设备认定，专门用于成人动脉瘤性蛛网膜下腔出血（aSAH）患者， 可有效降低脑血管痉挛的发生风险。

 

这也是目前全球首款针对该重症场景拿下FDA突破性认定的无创神经调控设备，为脑卒中重症诊疗开辟了全新技术路径。

 


在神经重症诊疗领域，动脉瘤性蛛网膜下腔出血（aSAH）是临床极为凶险的急性脑卒中病症。临床数据显示，aSAH患者出血后3至14天为脑血管痉挛高发窗口期，整体发病率可达30%-70%。

 

动脉瘤性蛛网膜下腔出血（aSAH）后引发并发症的核心病理逻辑

 

这一并发症是aSAH患者预后恶化的元凶。发病后脑血管持续性收缩，会直接造成脑组织缺血、二次脑损伤，不少患者即便挺过急性出血期，仍会遗留永久性神经功能障碍，严重时直接危及生命。

 

但临床可用的干预手段十分有限。

 

目前临床唯一指南推荐药物尼莫地平（一种钙通道阻滞剂）仅能缓解轻症症状，无法从根源阻断炎症致病机制，且对危重患者存在用药耐受差、低血压副作用等问题。

 

而球囊血管成形术等有创介入手段，仅适用于脑痉挛发作后的补救治疗，属于被动干预，无法实现早期预防，有创操作伴随手术创伤、血管损伤、术后感染等潜在风险，不适用于多数危重、昏迷患者。

 

截至目前，FDA尚未批准任何一款专用医疗器械，用于aSAH脑血管痉挛的前置预防，无创、安全、可快速落地的重症干预器械处于完全空白状态，临床未满足需求极为迫切。

 


V-Link的核心技术，来自圣路易斯华盛顿大学医学院神经外科Eric Leuthardt教授团队的长期研究。团队在临床中观察到，脑出血后的炎症反应是导致继发性脑损伤的关键因素。

 

但现有的抗炎手段——激素、免疫抑制剂——副作用大，剂量也难以精准把控，在ICU场景中并不理想。

 

这一临床痛点催生了V-Link的设计思路：通过物理刺激替代化学药物，激活人体自身的抗炎机制。

 

于是团队把目光投向了耳朵。

 

迷走神经耳支是迷走神经唯一延伸到体表的分支。这意味着，通过外耳皮肤施加电刺激，就能无创地“够到”这条掌管全身炎症反应的神经主干。

 

基于这一解剖学特征，V-Link被设计成一次性、可穿戴的耳戴式设备：无需开刀，不用植入，也不依赖药物。

 

那它具体怎么工作？

 

V-Link经耳迷走神经刺激作用机制示意图

 

aSAH发生后，颅内残留血液会触发机体免疫炎性级联反应，大量炎症因子释放，这是导致脑血管持续痉挛的核心诱因。

 

针对这一病例逻辑，V-Link通过耳部神经分支释放低能量精准电刺激，激活人体自身的抗炎通路，直接调控中枢和外周炎症反应，从病因上减少继发性脑损伤，而非单纯改善症状，实现从事后救治到事前预防的诊疗前置。

 

在产品设计层面，V-Link充分适配重症监护场景需求，采用一次性无线可穿戴设计，设备轻便易佩戴，ICU医护人员无需专项培训即可快速操作使用。

 

V-Link产品示意图

 

此外，标准化治疗流程极简。患者每日仅需接受2次、单次20分钟的耳部电刺激治疗，全程无创、无侵入操作，不干扰患者心率、血压、颅内压等核心监护指标，可以适配昏迷、危重等各类无法耐受有创操作的重症患者。

 


本次FDA突破性设备认定，核心依托一项权威随机、三盲临床试验数据支撑。研究纳入27例蛛网膜下腔出血成人患者，临床结果展现出突破性应用价值。

 

疗效层面，试验组患者中度至重度脑血管痉挛发生率，较对照组降低超40%，大幅减少重症并发症的出现，有效改善患者神经功能预后，并降低致残、致死风险。

 

安全性层面，整个试验周期内未发生任何设备相关不良事件，技术安全性得到初步权威验证，可与常规重症治疗方案兼容联用，无额外治疗风险。

 

除了临床获益，产品的卫生经济学价值同样突出。临床数据显示，接受V-Link干预的患者，30天相关住院费用下降20%，能够有效缩短ICU住院时长、减少重症医疗资源消耗，既能减轻患者就医负担，也能助力医疗机构优化重症医疗资源配置。

 


Aurenar成立于2020年，聚焦神经重症无创调控技术转化，背靠顶尖医学院科研团队，拥有扎实的技术转化基础。目前公司已完成2000万美元A轮融资，资金主要用于临床试验推进与FDA申报工作。

 

现阶段，企业已提交FDA研究性器械豁免申请，正式启动aSAH适应症关键性临床试验。按照企业规划，试验预计2027年完成，2028年递交De Novo上市申请，顺利获批后，V-Link将正式开启商业化落地。

 

与此同时，公司并未局限于单一适应症，正在持续拓宽产品边界，打造多场景协同的神经调控技术平台。

 

目前，V-Link针对缺血性卒中的临床试验已经正式启动，针对ICU应激性高血糖的管线也已完成布局，相关IDE申请预计2030年递交，逐步实现从单一脑出血治疗设备，向多重症场景神经调控平台的升级。

 

纵观全球非侵入式神经调控赛道，已有多款产品斩获FDA审批或突破性认定，但多数产品聚焦头痛、阿片戒断、外周血管疾病等轻症领域，针对神经重症、急性脑卒中的专项产品极为稀缺。

 

相较于Gamma Core、Masimo Bridge等同类经皮神经刺激产品，V-Link是少数明确卡位神经重症ICU场景的产品。获得突破性认定后，FDA将为其提供优先审评、专项沟通等政策支持，审评周期有望明显提速。

 

过去，面对aSAH后高达70%的脑血管痉挛风险，临床医生能做的只有“等痉挛发生后再抢救”。V-Link带来的改变在于，它让“预防”第一次成为可能。

 

当然，突破性认定不等于获批，关键性临床试验仍在推进中。但至少，ICU里有了一个值得期待的新选项。

title: How credentialing fears harm physician mental health [PODCAST]
summary: Subscribe to The Podcast by KevinMD. Watch on YouTube. Catch up on old episodes! Clinicians are treated as invincible, so when their own mental health suffers, many stay silent. Namit Choksi, a physic...
full_content:
Subscribe to The Podcast by KevinMD. Watch on YouTube. Catch up on old episodes!

Clinicians are treated as invincible, so when their own mental health suffers, many stay silent. Namit Choksi, a physician entrepreneur and health care executive, joins to explain how credentialing forms and a culture that treats wellbeing as a perk push doctors to hide depression, anxiety, and substance use until they reach a breaking point. You will hear why disclosing a behavioral health condition can threaten a clinician’s malpractice and life insurance and trigger a fit-for-duty review, why this fear creates a vicious circle that hospitals only notice once it is too late, and why losing a single clinician can cost more than a million dollars. Choksi also explains why legacy assistance programs fall short, with visit caps, long waits, and rotating counselors, and what confidential, barrier-free support looks like instead. This episode is based on his article “How credentialing and culture impact physician mental health,” published on KevinMD. You will come away understanding what institutions must change to keep their clinicians from suffering in silence.

Partner with me on the KevinMD platform. With over three million monthly readers and half a million social media followers, I give you direct access to the doctors and patients who matter most. Whether you need a sponsored article, email campaign, video interview, or a spot right here on the podcast, I offer the trusted space your brand deserves to be heard. Let’s work together to tell your story.

PARTNER WITH KEVINMD → https://kevinmd.com/influencer

SUBSCRIBE TO THE PODCAST → https://www.kevinmd.com/podcast

RECOMMENDED BY KEVINMD → https://www.kevinmd.com/recommended

Transcript

Kevin Pho: Hi, and welcome to the show. Subscribe at KevinMD.com/podcast. Today we welcome Namit Choksi. He is a physician entrepreneur and a health care executive. Today’s KevinMD article is “How credentialing and culture impact physician mental health.” Namit, welcome to the show.

Namit Choksi: Thank you, Kevin. It’s a pleasure to be here.

Kevin Pho: All right. Why don’t you briefly tell us a little about yourself, and then why you decided to share this particular article on KevinMD.

Namit Choksi: Absolutely. So, I’m Namit, originally from Mumbai, India, and a physician by training. I started training in obstetrics and gynecology, but as my dad jokes, I gave up too early.

I went to Harvard, got a degree in public health, and launched my own startup in the postpartum hemorrhage space alongside my mentor, Thomas Burke. I did my postdoc at Mass General and Harvard Medical School, again with the focus of reducing the cost of treating reproductive tract infections in women. Throughout my life, my focus has really been on bridging gaps in health care access using innovation and capital reallocation.

That journey took me to China, where I did a wonderful program called Schwarzman Scholars. I was keen to understand how China lifted about 800 million people out of poverty and gave both life insurance and health insurance to everyone. Recently, I joined Asklepian Group, a small growth partnership organization that invests in health care founders.

I became the CEO of Tend Health, a highly specialized provider of mental health care services for health care professionals. So that’s a quick introduction. I decided to write this article because I have always thought that, so far, wellness is looked upon as a perk rather than a need, and I honestly think the need is dire.

As physicians, people often think of us as invincible, as if we cannot be touched by any harm. The reality is that we see so much grief every day that it is difficult to remain untouched by all of it. So I decided to write about it, talk about it, and bring it into public view.

Kevin Pho: All right. One of the things I talk about when it comes to behavioral health issues in the physician community is that physicians, from the very beginning, are trained not to show vulnerability. So I think being able to talk about these behavioral health issues is slowly, hopefully, turning the corner. And the work you do will certainly help with that.

In your article, you make a connection between credentialing and physician mental health. For those who did not get a chance to read your article, tell us a little bit about it.

Namit Choksi: Sure. To be candid, Kevin, this piece really came from a frustration that I think many clinicians quietly share: that we often wait until people are already burned out, exhausted, and considering leaving medicine before we acknowledge that they truly need support.

As I said, at Tend Health, we work closely with clinicians across the country, and one thing became very clear to me: mental health support in health care is still too reactive. In my view, clinicians should not have to reach that breaking point before confidential, high-quality care becomes accessible and available to them.

So the article was really an attempt to reframe clinician wellbeing not as a wellness initiative, but as essential infrastructure for a functioning health care system. I often say that happier clinicians lead to happier patients, and happier patients lead to a healthier bottom line in health care, if that is what you care about.

Kevin Pho: Absolutely. And I think that focusing on physician mental health will hopefully prevent them from leaving the workforce, because it is much more expensive to replace a physician than it is to keep one you already have.

Namit Choksi: Yes. The average cost of losing a resident is about $500,000, and the average cost of losing a clinician is about $1.2 million. But beyond all of these numbers, what I really want is for clinicians to feel seen, felt, and heard, for lack of a better phrase, and I think that is what we aspire to do by providing them that support.

Kevin Pho: Now, specifically with credentialing, and by that I assume you mean when you apply for hospital privileges and things like that, that becomes a barrier for clinicians seeking help. For those who are not familiar with the physician credentialing process, can you explain why that is?

Namit Choksi: Absolutely. Let’s say you have a history of substance use, or a history of significant depression or anxiety. If that is actually put on paper, it can take away your ability to get malpractice insurance or life insurance, or you may not meet the fitness-for-duty assessment. In my view, at least as the CEO of a mental health company, it is a vicious circle.

Because clinicians are scared this will happen, they do not seek mental health care support. They get worse, and nobody detects it. It reaches a point where there is a domino effect and things start to fall apart. By the time people look into it, it is too late. So I think the credentialing issue is real, and changes are needed.

We are like any other human beings. I understand that if you are unfit for duty, you should not be seeing a patient, but at the same time, I should not be penalized time and again for seeking help.

Kevin Pho: And from what I understand, and you are certainly much more familiar with this world, some of these credentialing forms are removing questions about behavioral health. Tell us what the situation is like today. Is that commonplace? Are you seeing more of these credentialing forms remove behavioral health questions? What is happening today?

Namit Choksi: Great question, Kevin. Again, it is state specific. There is a lot of advocacy work going on through physician health organizations and clinician groups that are trying to remove these questions.

To be candid, we are far from done. I do think there are a few states that are much friendlier. I would like to think Massachusetts and a couple of others are much friendlier compared to the rest, but there are states where you would be heavily penalized for disclosing. So I would say it has at least started, but we have a long way to go. As they say, a long journey begins with a single step, and I think this is at least a step in the right direction.

Kevin Pho: Now, to be clear, if a physician has a behavioral health condition and has to truthfully disclose it on that form, can that harm them professionally? Tell us about some of the different scenarios you have seen when clinicians truthfully answer those behavioral health questions on credentialing forms.

Namit Choksi: Yeah, the short answer is yes. Let’s say a clinician comes forward and says, “I suffer from significant depression.” Then five follow-up questions go out, and I am fairly sure that out of those five, you will answer yes to at least one or two, and that gets you into a rollover effect. So yes, if you actually declare it, you technically get penalized.

Now, you get penalized in different ways. There are states that are much friendlier, where the response is, “OK, you can practice, but you need supervision and check-ins every now and then,” which I think is the right approach. But there are other states where the response is, “OK, we are going to take a step back. You pause, you get yourself treated, and then we need a fitness-for-duty assessment, and unless you are fit for duty you will not be able to see patients.” And this is also their livelihood. Now imagine that you are not getting a single paycheck while carrying an overwhelming amount of medical student loans, like most of us. So again, it gets into that vicious circle I am talking about.

Kevin Pho: And I am sure that incentive prevents a lot of physicians not only from seeking help, but also from being truthful on these credentialing forms. One of the things you wrote about in your article was legacy employee assistance programs. Tell us about the current situation with those, and why they may or may not be lacking in terms of what is really needed.

Namit Choksi: Great question, Kevin. I think traditional EAP programs technically just check those boxes, and I say that for a couple of reasons.

First, a lot of those EAPs have visit limits. A clinician often cannot do more than six visits annually in many EAP programs, so there is a visit cap. Second, it usually takes about two to three weeks to reach someone and get an appointment, and once you have that first appointment, it is not the clinician who will stick with you for the rest of your journey. So you have a constant change in clinicians.

Third, EAPs are often built on the assumption that you do not use them. They want lower utilization so that, at the end of the day, the cost makes sense. A lot of these EAP programs cost a couple of dollars per employee per month, whereas a solution like Tend is much more expensive, but we are not there to just check boxes. We are there to create an impact and to genuinely help.

And last but not least, the other big issue we face with traditional EAPs is availability. We are available late at night, early in the morning, and most weekends, which a traditional EAP is not. So we try to remove all the barriers to accessing mental health care support that a traditional EAP could not address.

If you ask me, an ideal solution would be to couple an EAP with a solution like Tend and get the best of both worlds, to the extent that it is cost permissible. That is why traditional EAPs do not really solve the problem on their own, and why you need a system that supports them on top. That is why we exist.

Kevin Pho: And I know that the solution you are offering is a virtual-first mental health support system, which gives you that access. Walk us through an ideal scenario of how your solution helps with some of the behavioral health issues clinicians face today.

Namit Choksi: Sure. As I said, Tend removes all the barriers to accessing mental health support. First, we do not put any caps on visits, so you can have an unlimited number of them. Second, about 95 percent of our clinicians are PhDs or PsyDs with extensive experience in mental health support for clinicians, so they have a lot of contextual knowledge. They are not going to tell a resident to take five days off or tell a physician to take a break from surgeries. They understand the realities.

Third, we are available late at night, early in the morning, and on weekends. We are present in 45 states and expanding to all 50 soon. And finally, we do not trigger insurance, and the reason is that we do not have to put anything on record. So people feel comfortable coming to us. It does not affect your ability to get malpractice insurance, and it does not affect your ability to get life insurance. So people feel a lot more comfortable coming to Tend Health.

Finally, we do everything. We are, as I said, the Cadillac version of mental health care support, if such a thing exists. We do psychiatry and medication management. We do coaching, couples counseling, and individual counseling. So you do not have to step outside the Tend ecosystem to get mental health care support. Everything you need is within that ecosystem.

Kevin Pho: What does that say about the system? One of the things you mentioned is that your solution does not trigger an insurance request, which in turn does not affect your malpractice or life insurance. What does it say about the system that you are offering this as a feature of the service you provide?

Namit Choksi: Well, what I am really trying to say is that we want clinicians to seek mental health support. We exist for them. Our patients are at the heart of our decision-making, and we want them to use us and to feel comfortable when they come to us. We do not want to just check boxes. We want to be there for them as a partner by their side every single time they reach out to us.

Kevin Pho: Now, let’s say medical institutions are listening to you right now. What recommendations would you give them if they want to take physician wellbeing seriously? What should they do next?

Namit Choksi: Great question, Kevin. One of the first things I would say is that people often think of mental health care support as a vitamin rather than a painkiller, and the reality is often the opposite. It is not a nice-to-have; I think it is a must-have. Obviously, I come from a place of bias, since I am the CEO of a mental health care company and a clinician.

But there are a couple of things I want to tell them. If you invest in your workforce, you will see the returns, and those returns can show up directly in your P&L, but also in your patient satisfaction, your clinician satisfaction, and your turnover rates. So I would say reach out to an organization such as ours. I know a lot of CEOs in the same space, and I often tell them that we are not competitors, we are collaborators, because the more attention this space gets, the better it is for all of us. So I would tell institutions to reach out to us at [email protected], and we would love to have a conversation and see how we could fit your needs.

But really, if you want to be there for your people, do not just check the box. It is a basic necessity. It is not a vitamin, it is not a nice-to-have, it is a must-have.

Kevin Pho: And specifically going back to credentialing, are you recommending that institutions simply remove questions about behavioral health from their credentialing forms? Would that be the most direct solution?

Namit Choksi: I would say, look, figure out a way to be supportive. I do not think clinicians would refuse to answer a question, even if it existed, if they knew there would not be dire repercussions. I think all that many of us as clinicians are asking for is to be treated as you would treat anyone else. Give us that support. We are human, so view us with that in mind.

I would say that taking those questions off would be a great interim solution. But the longer-term solution is actually figuring out the structure, so that even with those questions in place and a clinician answering them truthfully, we create a systemic change where it is not a taboo, where it is not looked down upon, and where clinicians are not penalized. So there needs to be a system-level change, but the near-term solution could be taking those questions away in the right spirit, and then figuring out how to build policies and procedures around them.

Kevin Pho: We are talking to Namit Choksi. He is a physician entrepreneur and health care executive. Today’s KevinMD article is “How credentialing and culture impact physician mental health.” Namit, let’s end with some take-home messages you want to leave with the KevinMD audience.

Namit Choksi: Yeah. I am really hoping that the health care organizations that lead health care through the next decade will be the ones that understand this is a must-have and not a nice-to-have. And to all the clinicians out there, keep up the good work, and more power to you. We are here to support you all day, every day.

Kevin Pho: Namit, thank you so much for sharing your perspective and insight. Thanks again for coming on the show.

Namit Choksi: Thank you, Kevin. It was a pleasure.

title: Why health care investing should start at the bedside
summary: The best investment I ever made didn’t start with a pitch deck. It started with a nurse spending two hours on a task that should have taken ten minutes. I was rounding as a hospitalist, and I no...
full_content:
The best investment I ever made didn’t start with a pitch deck. It started with a nurse spending two hours on a task that should have taken ten minutes.

I was rounding as a hospitalist, and I noticed, again, the amount of time clinical staff spent on manual equipment checks, compliance logs, and inventory reconciliation. It was the kind of operational friction that becomes invisible when you see it every day. You stop questioning it. You assume someone in administration knows about it and has decided the current process is good enough. Nobody had decided that. It was just how things were done.

Months later, when a physician in my network introduced me to a startup building a technology solution for exactly that problem, I didn’t need a market research report. I didn’t need a consultant to validate the clinical need. I had lived inside the pain point for years. I knew it was real because I watched it consume hours of clinical staff time every week, hours that could have been spent on patient care.

That investment became one of the strongest in my portfolio. And it taught me a principle I now apply to every deal: the highest-conviction health care investments don’t originate in boardrooms. They originate at the bedside.

The 3 a.m. thesis

There’s a specific type of insight that only physicians have, and it comes from working inside the health care system at hours when nobody from the business side is watching.

At 3 a.m., when you’re managing a crashing patient, you’re not thinking about market size. You’re thinking about what’s working and what isn’t. The EHR interface that adds clicks when you need speed. The communication gap between the ICU and the floor. The device that should integrate with your monitoring system but doesn’t. The medication that works clinically but takes 45 minutes to get through pharmacy because the ordering workflow was designed by someone who never placed an emergent order.

Every one of those frustrations is a potential investment thesis. Not a vague observation that health care is broken, but a specific, granular, workflow-level insight about where the system fails and what a solution would need to look like to actually get adopted.

The difference between a physician’s insight and a consultant’s insight is specificity. A consultant can tell you that hospital workflow inefficiencies cost the U.S. health care system billions annually. A physician can tell you that the specific bottleneck happens at shift change when the outgoing nurse has to reconcile a crash cart log that nobody trusts because it’s paper-based, and the incoming nurse has to redo the check because there’s no digital verification system. That level of granularity is where real products get built, and it only exists inside the heads of people who do the work.

Three patterns I’ve learned to recognize

After seven years of investing alongside a network of over 200 physicians, I’ve noticed that the strongest deals share a common origin story. They don’t start with a technology looking for a problem. They start with a clinical observation that someone refused to ignore.

The first pattern is the workaround signal. When you notice that clinical staff have built an informal system to compensate for a gap in their tools, a shared spreadsheet to track something the EHR doesn’t handle, a group text thread to communicate information that should flow through the system automatically, a manual log that everyone fills out but nobody trusts, that workaround is a product waiting to be built. The fact that people have gone to the trouble of creating a workaround means the pain is real and the existing tools are insufficient. I’ve funded companies that started as productized versions of workarounds physicians had been using for years.

The second pattern is the repeated complaint. Not the occasional grumble, but the frustration you hear from multiple clinicians across multiple institutions about the same problem. When a cardiologist in Baltimore and a hospitalist in Kansas City and a nurse manager in Houston independently describe the same workflow bottleneck, that’s market validation from the most informed user base in the world. No focus group produces signal that clean. When I hear the same complaint from three or more clinicians in our network, unprompted, in separate conversations, I start looking for companies solving that problem.

The third pattern is the near-miss. When a patient safety event almost happens because of a systems failure, equipment not where it should be, information not reaching the right person in time, a protocol gap that nobody noticed until it nearly caused harm, that near-miss identifies a problem with clinical urgency. Products that prevent near-misses don’t need a sophisticated sales pitch. They sell on patient safety, which is the one value proposition that every stakeholder in health care, physicians, administrators, insurers, and patients, agrees on.

Why boardroom-originated deals fail more often

The alternative is what I call boardroom origination: a non-clinical team identifies a market opportunity through research, builds a product based on consultant interviews, and tries to sell it into the clinical environment.

This approach isn’t inherently wrong. But the failure rate is higher when the founding insight doesn’t come from someone who has experienced the problem directly. The most common failure mode is building a product that solves the problem as described in a report rather than as experienced in practice. The report says hospitals struggle with inventory management. The reality is that hospitals have a dozen different inventory challenges, and the specific one painful enough to drive a purchasing decision varies by department, institution size, and payer mix. A physician who has lived inside that complexity knows which problem keeps the CNO up at night, because they’ve had the conversation in the hallway at 6 a.m.

What this means for you

If you’re a practicing physician, you’re generating investment-grade market intelligence every day without realizing it. The frustrations you experience, the workflow gaps, the technology failures, the communication breakdowns, are not just complaints. They’re data points that the health care investment industry needs and almost entirely lacks.

You don’t have to act on every one of them. But start paying attention to them differently. The next time you build a workaround for a tool that doesn’t work, ask yourself: Is someone building a solution for this? The next time a colleague at another institution describes the same frustration you’ve been living with, ask: Is this a pattern? The next time a near-miss happens because of a systems gap, ask: Who’s funding the fix?

The answers to those questions are where the best health care investments begin. Not in a boardroom. At the bedside. Where you already are.

Harsha Moole is an internal medicine-trained physician-scientist with more than 100 peer-reviewed publications, including work featured in the New England Journal of Medicine. After years of clinical practice and gastroenterology outcomes research, he made an unconventional transition from the bedside to the boardroom by founding PhysicianEstate, a health care-focused venture capital firm.

Over the past seven years, Dr. Moole has made 22 early-stage health care investments across digital health, medical devices, biotech, and therapeutics. He has also built a network of more than 200 physicians from institutions such as Johns Hopkins and Stanford who help source opportunities and provide clinical diligence before capital is deployed. His core thesis is that physician-scientists with firsthand clinical experience are uniquely positioned to identify health care investments that generalist investors often miss.

His research background is reflected in his publication record on Google Scholar, and he shares professional updates on LinkedIn.

title: What bariatric-friendly food really means
summary: Walk down any grocery aisle and you will see products covered in health claims: sugar-free, naturally sweetened, organic, gluten-free. For most shoppers, these labels are a minor annoyance. For a pati...
full_content:
Walk down any grocery aisle and you will see products covered in health claims: sugar-free, naturally sweetened, organic, gluten-free. For most shoppers, these labels are a minor annoyance. For a patient recovering from bariatric surgery, trusting the wrong one can mean an afternoon of dumping syndrome.

The term “bariatric-friendly” has no regulated definition. Any company can print it on a package. That leaves physicians, and eventually patients themselves, to figure out what the phrase should mean and to spot the products that use it without earning it.

The sugar-free trap

The most common problem I see is the protein bar loaded with sugar alcohols. Because it contains little actual sugar, it can be labeled “sugar-free,” and on paper it looks perfect for a bariatric patient. In reality, these bars regularly cause trouble. Dumping syndrome is one of the biggest issues post-op patients face, and heavy sugar alcohol consumption is a major cause that often gets overlooked.

The lesson patients learn early is simple. The front of the package is advertising. The back of the package is the truth.

What the numbers should look like

To be genuinely bariatric-friendly, a serving should provide around 15 grams of protein and stay under about 6 to 8 grams of total sugar.

Total is the key word. Many products advertise low “added sugars,” but what matters for a bariatric patient is the total sugar line on the nutrition panel.

The food also needs to be easy to digest. That rules out some tougher, fibrous meats and vegetables. These foods are perfectly healthy for most people, but they are the wrong choice for a stomach that has been surgically altered.

The same food can be right and wrong

Whether a food is appropriate also depends on where a patient is in recovery. Early on, textures need to be very smooth, and serving sizes grow slowly over the first few months.

Every patient moves through a structured diet progression with their surgeon or physician, beginning with clear liquids. Even a protein bar that meets every nutritional target does not belong in a patient’s diet until they have finished each phase of that progression. A food that is fine at month six can be a mistake at week two.

Labeling tricks

The most common thing that disqualifies a product marketed as bariatric-friendly is not the recipe. It is the label. Brands use phrases like “organic cane sugar” or “naturally sweetened” to look healthy. But a bariatric stomach does not care whether sugar is organic. It is still sugar, and too much of it will cause dumping or stop weight loss completely.

The same halo effect shows up elsewhere. Products with little else to brag about will display a “gluten-free” tag, even when the product obviously never contained gluten in the first place. Many shoppers see the tag and assume the food is healthy. “Organic” works the same way. Organic versions of products are generally better than the conventional ones, but that alone does not make a product good for you.

Few products are labeled specifically for bariatric patients, so it is hard to say exactly how many marketed items would pass a real clinical screen. What is clear is that companies are working harder than ever to make products look healthier than they are.

What about fast food?

Patients ask about fast food all the time, because life does not stop at the drive-through. Fast food is not the ideal choice for post-op bariatric patients, but it can be a viable option in a pinch if you choose wisely.

The same principles apply at the counter that apply in the grocery aisle. Choose grilled proteins over fried ones. Skip the bun, or order the meal “protein style” wrapped in lettuce. Avoid the biggest hidden risks: sugary drinks, milkshakes, special sauces, and large portions of carbs like fries and bread, which fill precious stomach space with little nutrition and can trigger dumping syndrome.

Portion control is the other half of the battle, and a few simple habits make it manageable. Order from the kids menu when you can. Eat half of the meal and save the rest for later. Eating a protein bar before the meal, or starting with a side of sliced tomatoes or cucumbers, will genuinely reduce how much you eat. Most chains now publish full nutrition information online, so patients can plan the order before they ever walk in. I keep a longer breakdown of chain-by-chain options in my guide to bariatric-friendly fast food.

There is a psychological piece here too. Many bariatric patients had an unhealthy relationship with fast food before surgery. Part of the work is recognizing trigger restaurants, identifying what the craving really is (protein, crunch, saltiness), and building new routines that satisfy it in a safer way.

The 10-second checklist

When a patient hands me a product and asks if it is OK, I do the same quick scan I use for my own shopping: calories, sugar, protein, then a glance at the first few ingredients on the list.

After a while, you develop a feel for the balance between those numbers. Bariatric patients pick up this skill quickly, because it becomes part of daily life. In the end, that skill protects them far better than anything a manufacturer prints on the front of a package.

Kevin Huffman is a board-certified bariatric physician and a nationally recognized leader in obesity medicine. After treating more than 10,000 patients over two decades of clinical practice, he founded American Bariatric Consultants, where he has mentored hundreds of health care professionals and helped them enter the bariatric medical community.

Dr. Huffman currently serves as medical director of AmBari Nutrition and sits on the corporate councils of both the American Society for Metabolic and Bariatric Surgery and the Obesity Action Coalition. A graduate of the Ohio University Heritage College of Osteopathic Medicine, he is a frequent consultant and lecturer on balancing high-quality bariatric treatment with the day-to-day realities of medical practice management.

Professional updates are available on LinkedIn.
